title: Re-rushing a traditional drop-in chair seat
subtitle: Strip old rush from a drop-in frame and weave a new rush seat using the figure-of-eight pattern
excerpt: How to strip the old rush from a traditional drop-in chair seat frame and re-weave a new rush seat using natural or paper rush in the standard figure-of-eight corner pattern, finishing at the centre of the seat.
sourceNotes: Victoria and Albert Museum furniture conservation notes (open access). Rural Crafts Association chair seating guidance. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: The standard weaving sequence for chair rushing, in which the rush cord makes a figure-of-eight around two adjacent rail corners at each pass: over the front rail, under and around the right rail, across the top, under and around the left rail, and over the front rail again — then repeated at the back. Working all four corners in sequence builds up the seat surface from the outside in, converging at the centre.

content.text: pattern works the same way for natural bulrush, twisted paper rush (seagrass), and fibre rush. Natural bulrush is now rare; paper and fibre rush are the standard for restoration work. Fibre rush at 4 mm or 6 mm diameter is the most practical choice: consistent, strong, and sold by the kilogram.

Fibre rush or paper rush cord (estimate by measuring the perimeter of the seat in metres and multiplying by the seat depth in metres, then by a factor of 15 to 20 depending on the cord diameter). A damp cloth or sponge to moisten the rush as you work. Thin offcuts of cardboard to stuff the seat pockets if needed. Strong twine for joining rush lengths at the underside of the seat.
Step 1, strip the old rush and prepare the frame

Cut the old rush away with the upholstery shears and pull out any remaining tacks or staples from the frame rails using the tack hammer. Sand the top rails lightly to remove any splinters or rough spots that would abrade the new rush. Check the frame for any loose joints; glue and clamp any that are wobbly. Allow glue to cure fully before starting to rush.

Tie the end of the rush cord to the left front rail (facing the seat), 20 mm from the left front corner. Working the figure-of-eight sequence: bring the cord over the front rail, under and around the front leg post, across the seat diagonally to the back right corner, over the back rail, under and around the back right leg post, back across to the back left corner, and so on. Keep the rush slightly damp as you work, damp rush is more flexible and beds down tighter. Pack each new strand against the previous one firmly using the regulator or a stick before moving to the next corner.

As the four corner triangles fill with rush, a square or rectangular open area remains in the centre of the seat. On a wider-than-deep seat, the front and back rails will fill before the side rails, leaving a strip to fill by weaving across the remaining gap without adding corner turns. Stuff the triangular pockets beneath the seat surface with folded cardboard as you go; this stiffens the finished seat and prevents the rush surface from sagging.
Step 4, finish and secure the end

When the last open space is filled, pass the cord end through the last few loops on the underside of the seat and tie off firmly with a square knot against the last strand. Trim the tail to 50 mm. Allow the rush to dry fully before use; it will tighten as it dries and the seat will firm up considerably.

attrs.symptom: Rush strands are uneven in width or texture across the seat surface
attrs.cause: Tension was not consistent throughout the weaving, or dry rush was used without dampening.
attrs.fix: Maintain even hand tension on every strand and work the rush damp. Pack each new strand firmly against the previous one before moving on.

attrs.symptom: Centre area cannot be fully closed at the finish
attrs.cause: The seat is wider than it is deep, requiring additional fills across the front-to-back direction before the side rails are reached.
attrs.fix: Make a series of straight passes across the open centre — over the front rail, under, back over the back rail — without the corner turns, until the remaining gap is the right size for the final complete rounds.
